text:
CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 59-year-old female with history of metastatic cutaneous melanoma, BRAF V600E-mutant, now on nivolumab maintenance. Prior disease sites included bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy (left dominant), multiple hepatic lesions, and subcutaneous nodules along the left flank. Prior imaging showed marked interval improvement. Surveillance.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following intravenous administration of 100 mL of Omnipaque-350 contrast. Axial images were obtained in the portal venous phase with coronal and sagittal reformats. Images were reviewed on PACS workstation.

COMPARISON: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast, prior study approximately 6 months ago.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The lungs are clear. No pulmonary nodules, masses, consolidation, or pleural effusions. Airways patent to the subsegmental level. No pneumothorax.

Mediastinum and Hila: Heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. Thoracic aorta is normal in caliber.

Axillary Regions: The left axillary lymph node that was previously enlarged and the subject of prior attention measures 0.5 cm in short axis on the current study, grossly stable compared to prior exam (previously 0.6 cm). This node has the appearance of a small benign-appearing or fibrotic lymph node. No new left or right axillary adenopathy identified. No chest wall masses.

Pleural Spaces: No pleural effusions or pleural thickening.

Chest Wall and Soft Tissues: No suspicious chest wall lesions or subcutaneous soft tissue masses.

---

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size with smooth contours. No focal hepatic lesions are identified. Areas previously noted in segments IV, VI, and VII — including a dominant lesion in segment VI and an additional lesion in segment IVa — are no longer visualized. The liver parenchyma enhances homogeneously. Intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated.

Gallbladder: Present, no stones, no wall thickening.

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation. No focal splenic lesions.

Pancreas: Normal appearing with no focal lesions or ductal dilation.

Adrenal Glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are unremarkable.

Kidneys: The kidneys are symmetric in size and enhancement. No hydronephrosis, nephrolithiasis, or renal masses.

Gastrointestinal Tract: Bowel loops are normal in caliber without wall thickening or obstruction. No free intraperitoneal air or fluid.

Retroperitoneum and Mesentery: No retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy. No vascular abnormality.

Abdominal Wall and Subcutaneous Tissues: No soft tissue masses or subcutaneous nodules along the left flank, which were previously identified and had resolved on prior imaging. This remains the case on the current study.

---

PELVIS:

Bladder: Normal in appearance. No wall thickening.

Uterus/Adnexa: Uterus and adnexa are unremarkable for patient age. No pelvic masses.

Pelvic Lymph Nodes: No pelvic or inguinal adenopathy. A previously noted subcentimeter right inguinal node is not discretely identified on this study.

Pelvic Soft Tissues and Bony Structures: No pelvic soft tissue masses. Bony structures of the pelvis are without suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesions.

---

BONE WINDOWS: The visualized thoracic, lumbar, and pelvic bones are without focal lytic or sclerotic lesions to suggest osseous involvement. Degenerative changes noted at multiple lumbar levels.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. No evidence of active metastatic disease on the current examination. Previously identified hepatic lesions are not visualized. Left axillary lymph node is subcentimeter and stable, most consistent with a benign/fibrotic lymph node in the appropriate clinical context.

2. The previously described subcutaneous nodules along the left flank remain absent.

3. No new suspicious lymphadenopathy, pulmonary nodules, hepatic lesions, or soft tissue masses identified.

4. Lungs clear. No pleural effusions.

5. Findings are consistent with sustained disease control in this patient with known history of metastatic melanoma on immunotherapy maintenance.

---

Dictated and electronically signed by: Attending Radiologist, Body Imaging
Reviewed by: Fellow, Diagnostic Radiology

cc: Oncology
